chunk:
CHUNK:048/048
Therefore, these nine crimes and fraud bear a logical subordinative relationship, forming statutory concurrence. Theoretically, this subordinative relationship is termed the relationship between special law and general law. Generally, in legislation, the general law stipulates exceptions—for instance, Article 267 on fraud states: “Where there are other provisions in this law, those provisions shall apply,” thereby excluding the application of this charge in favor of the other nine crimes. This is the principle of special law prevailing over general law. For instance, if fraudulent methods are used with the intent of illegal possession in fundraising, with a considerable amount, Article 192 on fundraising fraud should be applied instead of the general fraud charge. 2. **Overlapping Relationship in Statutory Concurrence**: This refers to cases where parts of the content in the constituent elements of two crimes overlap, and other parts do not. The overlapping content forms statutory concurrence. Due to the complexity of the overlapping content, handling methods also vary. For example, embezzlement and theft only overlap when a state functionary takes advantage of their position to steal public property. Both still include other non-overlapping scenarios, such as embezzlement involving methods like appropriation or fraud and theft including non-state functionaries or state functionaries not using their position to commit theft. From the perspective of theft, the distinction lies in the different identities of the perpetrators. In a broad sense, theft can encompass theft by special subjects. In this regard, these two crimes form a special-general law relationship, and the principle of special law prevailing over general law can be applied. However, in cases involving misappropriation of funds and illegal loans with off-book customer funds, the situation differs. The two crimes not only overlap in the perpetrator’s identity but also in behavior, yet do not form a special-general law relationship. Therefore, the principle of special law prevailing over general law cannot be applied. In such statutory concurrence cases without this relationship, the principle of heavier penalties over lighter penalties is applied. ### 4. Side Note: How to Compare Severe and Minor Crimes? Attentive readers will notice that in the section on crime quantity theory, we mentioned applying the principle of heavier penalties to cases of imaginary concurrence, connected crimes, and absorbed crimes. In the previous discussion, we also referred to "heavier law over lighter law." In the context of penalties, severe crimes and lighter crimes are equivalent to heavier law and lighter law respectively. So, what constitutes a severe crime and what constitutes a lighter crime? Or, how do we compare the severity of two charges? Different countries have different regulations for this. Some countries determine the severity of the crime based on the circumstances by first sentencing each crime separately and then giving a final judgment based on the most severe charge. However, this approach has flaws: on one hand, when sentences for multiple crimes are the same, determining the charge becomes difficult; on the other hand, a major flaw is the lack of statutory standards, which can lead to judicial errors. Most countries adopt the method of determining the crime after comparing statutory penalties. Generally, criminal law in each country prescribes various statutory penalties ranging from lighter to more severe. If different charges have different statutory penalty types, the one with a more severe penalty type is considered more severe. For instance, a charge that includes the death penalty is obviously more severe than one without it. In cases where the statutory penalty types are the same, the one with the longer maximum penalty is considered more severe; if maximum penalties are the same, the one with the longer minimum penalty is considered more severe. If both the statutory penalty ranges are identical, sentencing should be based on the crime with more serious circumstances or consequences (refer to Chen Xingliang, *General Theory of Criminal Law Application*, Vol. 1, Law Press, June 1999, pp. 660-662).
compressed_context:
STATUTORY-CONCURRENCE: NINE-CRIMES ∧ FRAUD HAVE SUBORDINATIVE RELATION  
RELATION = SPECIAL-LAW VS GENERAL-LAW (SPECIAL PREVAILS)  
ART267(FRAUD) STIPULATES “OTHER-PROVISIONS APPLY” ∴ ¬APPLY GENERAL-FRAUD  
EXAMPLE: FRAUDULENT-METHOD ∧ ILLEGAL-FUNDRAISING ∧ LARGE-AMOUNT → APPLY FUNDRAISING-FRAUD(ART192) ∧ ¬GENERAL-FRAUD  
OVERLAP-RELATION: CRIME-ELEMENTS PARTIALLY OVERLAP ∧ PARTIALLY DIVERGE → STATUTORY-CONCURRENCE  
VARIED-HANDLING ∵ OVERLAP-COMPLEXITY  
EMBEZZLEMENT VS THEFT: OVERLAP WHEN STATE-FUNCTIONARY ∧ POSITION-ABUSE → STEAL-PUBLIC-PROPERTY  
EMBEZZLEMENT INCLUDES MISAPPROPRIATION ∨ FRAUD  
THEFT INCLUDES NON-STATE-FUNCTIONARY THEFT ∨ STATE-FUNCTIONARY WITHOUT POSITION-ABUSE  
THEFT-PERSPECTIVE: DISTINCTION = PERPETRATOR-IDENTITY; THEFT-BROADLY INCLUDES SPECIAL-SUBJECTS  
∴ EMBEZZLEMENT-THEFT FORM SPECIAL-GENERAL RELATION → APPLY SPECIAL-PREVAILS  
CASE: MISAPPROPRIATION-OF-FUNDS ∧ ILLEGAL-LOANS(OFF-BOOK-CUSTOMER-FUNDS) OVERLAP IDENTITY ∧ BEHAVIOR  
¬SPECIAL-GENERAL RELATION → APPLY HEAVIER-PENALTY OVER LIGHTER  
NOTE: SEVERE-CRIME VS LIGHTER-CRIME = HEAVIER-LAW VS LIGHTER-LAW  
QUERY: HOW COMPARE-SEVERITY?  
SOME-JURISDICTIONS: SENTENCE EACH-CRIME SEPARATELY ∧ FINAL-JUDGMENT = MAX-SEVERE-CHARGE  
FLAWS: SAME-SENTENCE → AMBIGUOUS-CHARGE ∧ NO-STATUTORY-STANDARDS → JUDICIAL-ERRORS  
MOST-JURISDICTIONS: COMPARE STATUTORY-PENALTIES  
RULE1: PENALTY-TYPE RANKING → HIGHER-TYPE = MORE-SEVERE (DEATH-PENALTY > NO-DEATH)  
RULE2: IF TYPE = THEN MAXIMUM-PENALTY LONGER = MORE-SEVERE  
RULE3: IF MAX = THEN MINIMUM-PENALTY LONGER = MORE-SEVERE  
RULE4: IF RANGE = IDENTICAL → SENTENCE BASED ON CRIME WITH WORSE CIRCUMSTANCES/CONSEQUENCES [CHEN-XINGLIANG:1999-06; P660-662]
